nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving streams, ponds, and seasonal pools that may dry out periodically.

decorPreferences: fine gravel or sand with bulb planted on top, not buried
open background area with vertical clearance for long leaves

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as silver dollars or buenos aires tetras

summary: Sri Lankan bulb plant with distinctive crinkled, wavy, semi-transparent green leaves. Generally considered an easy background or specimen plant for medium to large tanks.
careNotes: Aponogeton crispus is a hardy bulb plant from Sri Lanka with striking crinkled, wavy leaves that tend to reach 30-50 cm tall, making it well suited as a background or solitary focal specimen in tanks of around 120 L or larger. It is generally advised to plant the bulb on top of the substrate (or only half-buried) rather than fully covering it, to avoid rot. The plant has been reported to do well across a fairly wide range — temperatures around 22-28 °C, pH roughly 6.0-7.5, and soft to moderately hard water (about 2-12 dGH). It is commonly considered easier than its relative A. madagascariensis (fw-plant-012) and is often recommended for beginners. CO2 injection is not required but can boost growth alongside a nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs. Specimens may go through a natural dormancy where the leaves die back for several weeks; do NOT discard the bulb — leave it in the substrate and new leaves typically resprout within 2-8 weeks. Sources disagreed somewhat on light level (low to high) and pH (one source listed 5-6.5, others 6.0-7.5); averaged values are reported here, with medium light selected as a sensible default.
breedingNotes: Primarily propagates by producing flower spikes above the water surface, which can self-pollinate to set seed; seedlings can be grown on separately. Daughter bulbs occasionally form on mature plants and can be separated. Flowering and seed production are reported to be uncommon under typical aquarium conditions.
